narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 55% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your solid sixth-decile ranking in Year 1 grades (a bit above the typical student's fifth-decile average), but a concerning second-decile standing in Year 3 (well below the average fifth-decile level). Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 is right around the average, and your middle-family-income background aligns with many applicants. However, your LSAT score of 21.30 stands out as notably lower than the typical 36.70 average, which played a significant role. As a full-time female student from a Black background—groups that make up smaller shares of the applicant pool (full-time is common at 93%, females 44%, Black students just 5%)—these details were factored in too.

The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 academic decile, which had the biggest influence in pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure compared to stronger performers. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, which also nudged the odds toward failure rather than success. Your LSAT score ranked third in impact, similarly contributing to the concern about passing the bar. Family income followed closely, adding a slight push in the same direction, while your racial background had a comparable but smaller effect toward the failure prediction. Your undergraduate GPA came after that in influence, also leaning negative, with your full-time status having minimal pull toward failure. Interestingly, your gender as female was the one factor that slightly favored a passing prediction, helping to offset some of the risks.

In comparison to typical students who pass the bar on the first try, your profile shows strengths like average UGPA and Year 1 performance, but the dips in later academics and LSAT raised red flags that outweighed them. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can control: consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above average, as it's a key influencer and retakes are common. Strengthening your study habits or seeking tutoring could help demonstrate better late-stage academic momentum, even if through additional coursework or certifications. Building bar exam prep experience, like targeted practice tests, might also reassure admissions teams in future applications. You're close—small improvements in these areas could tip the scales toward approval. Keep pushing; many students turn things around with focused effort.
